content.name: Rule 110
intro: is a one-dimensional binary cellular automaton rule introduced by Stephen Wolfram in 1983.
It is known to be Turing-complete,
meaning that it can simulate any computation that can be performed by a Turing machine.
This exercise is thus a proof of Turing-completeness of combinatory logic,
Given a linked list of bits, encoded as t=K and f=KI,
produce a list of the same length containing the next iteration of the automaton,
Lists are encoded as (V x (V y (V z nil))), where V x y z = z x y.
Assume the cells before and after the list are always 0.
cases: r110 (V f (V f (V f (V t (V f (V f (V t (V t (V f (V t (V t (V t (V t (V t nil))))))))))))))
(V f (V f (V t (V t (V f (V t (V t (V t (V t (V t (V f (V f (V f (V t nil))))))))))))))
r110 (V f (V f (V f (V t (V t (V t (V f (V t (V t (V t nil))))))))))
(V f (V f (V t (V t (V f (V t (V t (V t (V f (V t nil))))))))))
